title: Gynecology: Principles and Practice
abstract:
Practical detail is sometimes omitted.
On page 803, in discussing the evalua¬
tion of a hot nodule, the author suggests
"a short course of suppressive therapy
followed by a suppression scan to con¬
firm that the nodule has functional au¬
tonomy." This statement is vague: How
long should the patient undergo ther¬
apy?
What preparation
and dosage
should be used? Which tracer is pre¬
ferred? Similarly, on page 850 mention
is made of the increasing problem of the
incidental finding of adrenal tumors on
computed tomographic scanning of the
abdomen. The reader is advised to "do a
battery of screening tests, previously
described in this section." A great num¬
ber of adrenal tests are, in fact, de¬
scribed in this section. What particular
tests does the author suggest? If this
text is to be useful to the student and
resident,
more clinical detail is
re¬
quired.
Occasionally there is an annoying re¬
dundancy of information. "Thyroid Bi¬
opsy" is treated on pages 738 and 802,
"Incidental Adrenal Tumors" on pages
850 and 979, and "Treatment of Prolac-
tinomas" on pages 408 and 608.
The text is at times difficult to read. I
would like to see clinical algorithms that
are easy to follow and thus beneficial to
the young clinician.
In summary, despite some shortcom¬
ings, the seventh edition of Williams
Textbook ofEndocrinology continues to
be a valuable reference for the student,
resident, and practicing endocrinolo¬
gist. The Williams textbook remains the
standard by which other endocrine
texts must be measured.
Sheldon S. Stoffer, MD
Southfield, Mich
Surgery
Basic Surgery, edited by John A. McCredie, ed 2;
656 pp, with illus, $29.95, New York, Macmillan
Publishing Co Inc, 1986.
This 656-page paperback second edi-
tion is an updated version of the original
1977 publication. As stated by the edi-
tors, it is "prepared as a textbook and
supplement for the medical student and
surgical resident."
For ease of reference the book has
been divided into four units and com-
prises 35 chapters. Unit 1 discusses
"Basic Considerations" and covers met-
abolic responses, monitoring, fluid and
electrolyte balance, immune responses,
tissue healing, surgical infections, and
cancer. The presentations are abbrevi-
ated, but for the audience intended it
serves very well. The chapters on moni-
toring and immune response are par-
ticularly well done.
Unit 2, covering "Total Care," con-
tains basic information and allows the
student to become familiar with new
terms and concepts. Units 3 and 4, on
"Principles of General Surgery" and
"Principles of Speciality Surgery," pro-
vide a general discussion of the various
body systems, emphasizing the princi-
ples of therapy rather than detailing
surgical technique. The surgical spe-
cialities are broadly discussed and pre¬
sented in
an easily read style that
should be informative for the medical
student. However, obstetrics and gyne¬
cology, ophthalmology, and otolaryngol-
ogy are not discussed. Hundreds of
illustrations add greatly to an under¬
standing of the material. All in all I
believe the authors have satisfied the
avowed purpose ofthe book: to aid medi¬
cal students.
W. G. Abel, MD
SUNY
Stony Brook, NY
Antimicrobials
The Antimicrobial Agents Annual/I, edited by
Phillip K. Peterson and Jan Verhoef (The Anti-
microbial Agents Annual, J. F. Acar et al, eds), 582
pp, with illus, $69, New York, Elsevier Science
Publishing Co Inc, 1986.
This multiauthored work (68 distin-
guished authors) consists of two major
sections. The first contains 35 chapters
dealing with antibacterial, antifungal,
antiparasitic, and antiviral agents. The
second, "Current Topics in Antimicro-
bial Therapy," consists of 12 well-writ-
ten reviews.
The editors of this ambitious work
note that it is to be the first in a series of
annual volumes designed to keep pace
with the rapid advances in antimicrobial
chemotherapy. They disclaim any re-
sponsibility in providing a comprehen-
sive textbook; instead, their goal is to
assess new information critically and to
place it in current perspective. Despite
its impressive size (582 pages), the book
may not be usable as a reference be-
cause basic information is not always
included.
The chapter on cephalosporin (San-
ders and Sanders) is relatively large (25
pages) compared with the others. A
concise overview ofnewer information is
presented with a bibliography of 150
references\p=m-\allfrom 1980 to 1985! For
some antimicrobial classes in which
there have been fewer advances, the
chapters, by and large, consist of mate¬
rial available in standard pharmacology
textbooks.
A certain unevenness and deviation
from a standard format is unavoidable in
any multiauthored text. For example,
"mechanism of action" is explicitly la¬
beled
as
a separate section in some
chapters, integrated into the introduc-
tion in other chapters, and even omitted
in a few chapters.
The chapters on quinolones, ß-lacta-
mase inhibitors, acyclovir, and ribavirin
are reasonably comprehensive, while
the chapters
on rifampin,
the
car-
bapenems, and praziquantel are disap¬
pointingly short. Monobactams are not
discussed as a group, although aztreo-
nam is covered reasonably well. The six
chapters on antiparasitic agents pro¬
vide an updated and needed review of
this group.
Despite the obvious strengths of this
section, I remain unconvinced that a
hardcover volume is an effective format
for cataloging new changes. Basic infor¬
mation on mechanisms of action, phar¬
macology, and indications is needed, if
only to provide a framework for newer
information. As a result, chapters in the
first section are a curious hodgepodge of
old and new information. I wonder, with
an upcoming Antimicrobial Agents An-
nual-II, what information in volume 1
will be retained and duplicated? The
usefulness of the first section of volume
1 will be dependent on the frequency
and the format of the succeeding vol¬
umes.
The second section dealing with cur¬
rent topics (217 pages) is the strength of
this book. It includes "Antibiotic-In¬
duced
Hemostatic
Abnormalities"
(Johnson) and "Treatment of Methicil-
lin-Resistant Staphylococci"
(Rahal).
Review topics
include
antimicrobial
therapy for acquired immunodeficiency
syndrome-related
infections
(Wofsy)
and an updated survey of antibiotic dos¬
ing in renal insufficiency (Matzke and
Keane). The concise review on antibiotic
tissue penetration (Peterson and Gerd-
ing) is particularly well done.
Overall, the book is excellent and a
desirable addition to any medical li¬
brary. It is also recommended for phar¬
macists and physicians with special in¬
terests
in
infectious
diseases
and
antibiotics.
Victor L. Yu, MD
University of Pittsburgh
Gynecology
Gynecology: Principles and Practice, by Robert
W. Kistner, ed 4; 716 pp, with illus, $65, Chicago,
Year Book Medical Publishers Inc, 1986.
Gynecology Principles and Practice,
fourth edition, by Robert W. Kistner,
MD, is a well executed textbook, which
affords the reader with a nicely bal-
anced overview of the basic science of
gynecology coupled with information on
clinical diagnosis and management.
The first half of the book is laid out by
organ system, with each chapter con-
taining subchapters on that particular
Downloaded From: http://jama.jamanetwork.com/ by a Simon Fraser University User  on 06/02/2015
 organ's pathological entities, after
covering the basics with the normal
anatomy, embryology, and physiology of
that organ. The second half of the book
deals mainly with more systemic or
nonorgan-oriented gynecologic disease,
and these chapters go into much more
depth on each pathological entity. The
book is somewhat reminiscent of The
Textbook of Gynecology by Novak in
both its format and its emphasis on
histological pathology.
This book gives an up-to-date and
concise overview ofgynecology that can
be appreciated in many forms: the third\x=eow-\
and fourth-year medical student will
find it useful for an overview of the
specialty, and it should certainly help
the student on his or her clinical rota¬
tion, as well as aid in studying for that
portion of the National Boards. The
house officer should find it useful in
studying for the in-training standard¬
ized examinations, and it may also come
in handy as a generalized review for the
Gynecology Specialty Board, presum¬
ing the examinee has previously done
in-depth preparation.
Although the book touches on man¬
agement ofclinical entities, it is not very
helpful about in-depth patient treat¬
ment situations. However, a nice, short,
concise description of each pathological
entity is provided. What is very helpful,
however, is the arrangement of each
chapter, which describes the diagnosis
of each entity by the logical sequence of
history, physical examination, and diag¬
nostic procedures.
Probably, the greatest shortcoming of
the book is that 15 of the 16 contributing
authors are Harvard University affili¬
ated, and all 16 are from the north¬
eastern United States. Hence, the pro¬
tocol described in the book may not
indeed reflect the difference in geo¬
graphic practices of gynecology in this
country.
Dave E. David, MD
University of California at Irvine
Newport Beach
Software
Nephrology
Nephros: A Renal Clearance Analysis program,
by Michael S. Simonson, one diskette for IBM PC or
Apple lie and lie; documentation, 20 pp manual,
with
illus,
$60,
Amsterdam,
the
Netherlands,
Elsevler
Science
Publishers
BV;
Cambridge,
United Kingdom, Elsevler-BIOSOFT (69 Hills Rd,
CB2 1LA).
This software program calculates re¬
nal clearance from the standard urinary
volume/plasma concentration ratio or
from values obtained by a constant infu-
sion technique with substances such as
inulin. It is user-friendly and mostly
self-explanatory. Directions are clear to
follow.
The first screen offers entry to sev¬
eral activities in a menu with choices for
entering and redisplaying data, calcu¬
lating clearances, and printing or re¬
displaying previous data files. The next
screen has blank spaces for entering the
name of the patient, the date, the com¬
pound used, and the number of clear¬
ance periods. The operator types in the
answer to the first question and presses
the "enter" key; then the cursor moves
to the next space and question. There
are also spaces for entering the kind of
perfusate used and the rate ofperfusion
if a constant infusion method is used.
These may be bypassed if desired. The
third screen asks for urine volume, and
urine and plasma concentration. The
fourth screen displays the previously
entered values in a chart, with columns
for volume, time, and urine and plasma
concentration. These data may be
printed or stored on a disk. Then the
program returns to the first screen, and
it is only at this point that one proceeds
to the actual calculation of the clear¬
ance. Another chart then displays the
clearance values, which may be printed
in a report. Up to 11 clearance periods
may be calculated at one time.
The program will calculate the previ¬
ously entered data even if they are not
stored on a disk. It is important to be
consistent with units: concentration is
always recorded in micrograms per mil-
liliter, and the program cannot convert
from one unit of measure to another.
The program is intended to be used as a
tool for those who must calculate clear¬
ances frequently,
more likely in re¬
search than in clinical medicine, where a
calculator may be all that is required. It
does not include a tutorial or educa¬
tional section.
Nephros, written in BÁSICA, is
available in a version that can be used
with the IBM PC and the Apple lie or
He personal computers. These must be
equipped with a minimum of 32K mem¬
ory. The authors assume that the user
has had no previous experience with
computers. They provide a clearly writ¬
ten accompanying manual that starts
with step-by-step instructions for pre¬
paring the program disk for use in the
personal computer without the use of
the "system disk." The manual also gives
instructions for making a backup copy of
the program on another disk.
Nephros seems somewhat outdated
and lacks the sophistication ofcurrently
available software. When compared
with most popular spread-sheet or data¬
base programs, it seems cumbersome.
The menu cannot be bypassed for rapid
calculations or data entry, nor can the
program be used to print graphs or
extend the formula to other uses, such
as calculating free-water clearance.
There is a bug: given a plasma value of0,
the program defies the laws of mathe¬
matics and will print a clearance value of
1.7E + 37 mL/min, which probably rep¬
resents the largest number in the com¬
puter memory divided by the smallest
number. This discrepancy could have
been rectified easily by displaying an
error message. It is not at all obvious,
then, who would benefit from using
Nephros. It is too cumbersome for the
clinician, too simple for the investigator,
and provides too little insight for the
medical student.
Nadine Bazilinski, MD
Cook County Hospital
Chicago
Books Received
Alcohol and Drug Dependence
Opioid Peptides: Medicinal Chemistry, edited by
Rao S. Rapaka, Gene Barnett, and Richard L.
Hawks (NIDA Research Monograph 69), 376 pp,
Rockvllle, Md, National Institute on Drug Abuse,
1986.
Opioid
Peptides:
Molecular
Phrarmacology,
Biosynthesis, and Analysis, edited by Rao S.
Rapaka and Richard L. Hawks (NIDA Research
Monograph 70), 414 pp, with ¡llus, Rockvllle, Md,
National Institute on Drug Abuse, 1986.
Strategies for Research on the Interactions of
Drugs of Abuse, edited by Monique C. Braude
and Harold M. Ginzburg (NIDA Research Mono¬
graph 68), 231 pp, with ¡llus, Rockvllle, Md, National
Institute on Drug Abuse, 1986.
Anesthesiology
Drug Interactions in Anesthesia, by N. Ty Smith
and Aldo N. Corbascio, ed 2; 482 pp, $47.50,
Philadelphia, Lea & Febiger, 1986.
Cancer
Advances in Immunity and Cancer Therapy, vol
2, edited by P. K. Ray, 300 pp, $55, New York,
Springer-Verlag NY Inc, 1986.
Cancer in Ontario 1985, by Ontario Cancer Treat¬
ment and Research Foundation, 277 pp, with ¡llus,
Toronto, Ontario, Ontario Cancer Treatment and
Research Foundation, 1986.
Current Hematology and Oncology, vol 4, edited
by Virgil F Fairbanks, 405 pp, with ¡llus, Chicago,
Year Book Medical Publishers Inc, 1986.
Gastrointestinal Oncology, by John W L Fielding
and T J. Priestman, 330 pp, with ¡llus, $52.50,
Philadelphia, Lea & Febiger, 1986.
Immunology and Cancer, vol 38, edited by
Margaret L. Krlpke and Philip Frost (symposium,
Houston, February-March 1985), 304 pp, with illus,
$47.50, Austin, Tex, University of Texas Press, 1986.
